items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a likely RCID fit across Digital Humanities / Design.
items.fullDescription: "[...]anomal ne signifie autre chose qu'insolite, inaccoutumé. On doit donc entendre d'une manière générale, par un être anomal, un être qui s'éloigne par son organisation de la grande majorité des êtres auxquels il doit être comparé[...]" (Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, Histoire générale et particulière des anomalies chez les hommes et les animaux , 1837) Conceived as the concluding conference of the PRIN project Tea-Med - Theatre and Medicine, Anatomies of Anomaly: Trajectories of Deformity across Literature, Performance, and Clinical Discourse aims to explore the category of the anomalous through its historical, cultural, medical, literary, and performative articulations. Drawing on the definition formulated by Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire - for whom the anomalous coincides with that which appears "unusual" or "uncommon," removed from the majority of bodies and forms deemed regular - the conference aims to expand this category to encompass everything that exceeds normative paradigms, escapes fixed classifications, and resists the systems of control to which it is subjected. In this sense, the anomalous does not merely designate bodily or physiological deformity, but also every form of alterity that destabilises the boundaries of the familiar: identities perceived as eccentric in relation to dominant models; female subjectivities that exceed or subvert historically imposed social and cultural roles; linguistic, narrative, and textual singularities; excluded or marginalised voices; experiences of madness, trauma, and disability; subversive literary and editorial practices; and bodies and minds that challenge the very notion of "normality". As Rosemarie Garland Thomson observes, "The unexpected body fires rich, if anxious, narratives and practices that probe the contours and boundaries of what we take to be human" (Freakery, 1). The conference therefore aims to interrogate those forms of representation, narration, and discourse that have contributed - and continue to contribute - to destabilising and reshaping the category of the human itself; discourses, cultural practices, and modes of knowledge that challenge the boundaries of the representable, the intelligible, and the normative. From this perspective, the conference seeks to foster an interdisciplinary dialogue among scholars working in the history of medicine, literary and linguistic studies, theatre and performance studies, disability studies, gender studies, queer studies, philosophy, psychoanalysis, cultural history, visual arts, and related fields. Particular attention will be devoted to theatre as a privileged site for the exhibition, negotiation, and representation of anomaly: a space in which the deformed, pathological, eccentric, or marginal body is observed, medicalised, spectacularised, or rewritten through new narrative and political possibilities. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: • The monstrous body: teratology, eugenics, and studies of physical alterity • Anomalous minds: madness, trauma, and psycho-physical pathologies • Disability as anomaly: historical, social, and medical representations of disability • Gendered anomaly: queer bodies, performativity, and non-normative identities • Anomaly as social role: disguise, cross-dressing, and the subversion of gender norms • Spectacles of anomaly: freak shows, circuses, and fairs • Anomalous language: linguistic, narrative, dramaturgical, and textual anomalies • The anomalous as archive: case studies, medical documentation, archival gaps, and recording practices • The anomalous as a tool of social control: medicalisation, marginalisation, and the disciplining of bodies • Disturbing anomalies: the grotesque, the pathological, and the uncanny • Anomalous performances: voice, gesture, stage presence, and eccentric corporeality • The anomalous in visual and media cultures: visual arts, cinema, media, and performance • Anomalous spaces: ecologies, environments, and landscapes of deformity • Spiritual anomalies: possession, ecstasy, and altered states • The anomalous beyond the human: posthumanism, alterity, and the redefinition of the norm • Anomalous societies: non-Eurocentric cultural practices and social structures Submission Guidelines The conference welcomes contributions spanning all historical periods and geographical contexts, with a particular emphasis on comparative and interdisciplinary approaches. The call for papers is open to PhD students and early career researchers interested in contributing to the conference discussion. Proposals, in Italian, English, or French, must be submitted by 15 August 2026 to convegnoanomalie.unifi@gmail.com. Submissions should be sent as a .pdf or .docx file named "Surname_AnatomiesConference" and must include the title, an abstract (maximum 300 words), 5 keywords, 5-7 bibliographical references relevant to the proposed topic and a short bio-bibliographical note (maximum 150 words). Authors are also required to specify their institutional affiliation, disciplinary field, and the language of their presentation (Italian, English, or French). All proposals will be evaluated by the Scientific Committee. Notification of acceptance will be communicated by 30 September 2026. Each speaker will have 20 minutes for their presentation. The conference will take place in person on Thursday 26 and Friday 27 November 2026 at the University of Florence. Further details regarding the venue and conference programme will be provided upon confirmation of participation. Participation in the conference is free of charge and accommodation expenses for one night will be reimbursed. Participants are responsible for their own meals and travel costs.

items.fullDescription: Transnational Feminisms have emerged out of the need for women’s groups to address oppression that crosses national borders and to build solidarities rooted in an exploration of the articulation between the local and the global. These forms of feminisms examine the ways in which women’s groups transcend location beyond national boundaries to address their political and ideological struggles, exploring the interrelationships between discrete factors such as caste, class, ethnicity, sexuality, ability, age, and multiple other positionalities and contexts of inequalities as they overlap and are shaped by neoliberal economic and political forces, patriarchies and various other systems of stratification.

items.fullDescription: Fantasy has long explored lifeworlds and paradigms outside of societal norms. Orlando, Virginia Woolf’s gender-fluid protagonist, declares, ‘I'm sick to death of this particular self. I want another.’ World myths, legends, folk tales, and fairy tales are early promoters of gender-fluidity, populated by the likes of Inanna/Ishtar; Hermaphroditus, the offspring of Hermes and Aphrodite; androgynous Dionysus; Ardhanarishvara; Ometeotl; Guanyin; cross-dressing thunder and trickster gods; heartsick seafaring maidens disguised as sailors; and the mercurial ontologies of the Fae. In this issue we will explore how gender is portrayed and explored in Fantasy.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers for an anthology on the topic Reflections on Authorship in Contemporary Drama and Theatre Editors: Kai Bremer, Anton Bröll, Norbert Otto Eke Published as part of the DFG project "Reflections on Authorship in Contemporary Drama" at Freie Universität Berlin (See below for the German version) In his 1956 work on the theory of modern drama, Peter Szondi took as his starting point the "absence" of the author in drama. Looking back at the history of drama over the past few decades, this category seems to be a thing of the past. In particular, an autofictional character bearing the author's name, or an "I" that appears to refer to the author as a real person, serves to establish an explicit reference to the author. In the German-speaking world, Peter Handke, Heiner Müller, and Elfriede Jelinek can be considered the pioneers of this practice, which serves to reflect on and stage their own authorship. Since the 2000s, however, a significant increase in this mode of writing has also been observed internationally. This is equally evident in stage productions and performances in which the staging of the author's persona either suggests authenticity or is critically undermined through theatrical means. This may be accompanied by a reflection on the director's position as well as that of other theatre practitioners, whose relationship to the author or to the written text becomes a thematic focus. At the same time, individual concepts of authorship are challenged by collective or collaborative forms, whose structures and legitimacy may themselves become subjects of reflection. This call for papers invites contributions in German or English that examine reflections on authorship in contemporary drama and theatre beyond the German-speaking context . Examples of works that could be examined include Sarah Kane's 4.48 Psychosis, Michael Redhill's Goodness, Ella Hickson's The Writer, Olivier Py's Le Soleil, or Rébecca Chaillon's Carte Noire nommée Désir. Contributions that move beyond Eurocentric and Western-centred perspectives are particularly welcome. The following questions may guide the contributions: What strategies of reflection on authorship are employed, and what functions do they serve? What role do autobiographical references to the author, director, or other theatre practitioners play? How are these positions related to one another, and what relationship can be identified with other works or self-presentations in the media? In which aesthetic tradition can these meta-reflections be situated? To what extent are other authorial positions or concepts of authorship taken up affirmatively or critically? Are there culturally, institutionally, or media-specific forms of authorship reflection? Please submit your abstract (max. 1 page) for a paper in German or English, along with a short biography , by August 31, 2026 , via email to the editors: anton.broell@fu-berlin.de Completed contributions (35,000-50,000 characters including spaces) should be submitted by mid-2027. The volume is planned for publication in the series "Drama zwischen Text und Bühne" (Brill / Fink). Call for Papers für einen Sammelband zum Thema Reflexionen von Autor:innenschaft in Drama und Theater der Gegenwart Herausgeber: Kai Bremer, Anton Bröll, Norbert Otto Eke Publikation im Rahmen des DFG-Projekts „Autorschaftsreflexionen in der Gegenwartsdramatik" an der Freien Universität Berlin Peter Szondi ist 1956 in der Theorie des modernen Dramas von der „Abwesenheit" der Autor:in im Drama ausgegangen. Bilanziert man die Geschichte des Dramas der letzten Jahrzehnte, scheint diese Kategorie Geschichte zu sein. Insbesondere eine autofiktionale Figur, die den Autor:innen-Namen trägt, oder ein ‚Ich', das auf die empirische Person zu rekurrieren scheint, dienen der Herstellung eines exponierten Autor:innen-Bezugs. Als Wegbereiter dieser Einschreibung, die der Reflexion und Inszenierung der eigenen Autor:innenschaft dient, können im deutschsprachigen Raum vor allem Peter Handke, Heiner Müller und Elfriede Jelinek gelten. Seit den 2000er-Jahren lässt sich aber auch international eine signifikante Zunahme dieser Schreibweise beobachten. Dies zeigt sich ebenso in Inszenierungen und Performances, in denen die Vergegenwärtigung der Autor:innen-Persona entweder Authentizität suggeriert oder durch szenische Mittel analytisch gebrochen wird. Hinzutreten kann die Reflexion der Regieposition sowie weiterer Theaterschaffender, deren Verhältnis zur/zum Autor:in oder zum Geschriebenen thematisch wird. Zugleich werden individuelle Konzepte von Autor:innenschaft durch kollektive oder kollaborative Formen infrage gestellt, deren Konstellation und Legitimation ebenfalls Gegenstand der Reflexion sein können. Mit diesem CFP werden deutsch- oder englischsprachige Beiträge für einen Sammelband erbeten, die sich der Metareflexion von Autor:innenschaft im nicht-deutschsprachigen Gegenwartsdrama und -theater widmen. Untersucht werden könnten beispielsweise Sarah Kanes 4.48 Psychosis, Michael Redhills Goodness, Ella Hicksons The Writer, Olivier Pys Le Soleil oder Rébecca Chaillons Carte Noire nommée Désir. Besonders willkommen sind Beiträge, die den eurozentrischen und westlich geprägten Blickwinkel erweitern. Folgende Fragen können die Beiträge leiten: Welche Verfahren der Autor:innenschaftsreflexion werden eingesetzt, und welche Funktionen erfüllen sie? Welche Rolle spielen autobiografische Bezüge zu Autor:in, Regie oder weiteren Theaterschaffenden? Wie werden diese Positionen zueinander in Beziehung gesetzt, und welches Verhältnis lässt sich zu anderen Arbeiten oder medialen Selbstinszenierungen bestimmen? In welche ästhetische Tradition lassen sich die Metareflexionen einordnen? Inwiefern werden andere Autor:innenpositionen oder Konzepte von Autor:innenschaft affirmativ oder sich abgrenzend aufgegriffen? Treten kulturelle, institutionelle oder mediale Spezifika der Autor:innenschaftsreflexion hervor? Bitte senden Sie Ihr Abstract (max. 1 Seite) für einen Beitrag auf Deutsch oder Englisch sowie eine Kurzbiografie bis zum 31.08.2026 per E-Mail an die Herausgeber: anton.broell@fu-berlin.de Die Beiträge (35.000-50.000 Zeichen inkl. Leerzeichen) sind bis Mitte 2027 einzureichen. Die Publikation ist in der Reihe „Drama zwischen Text und Bühne" im Verlag Brill und Fink geplant. Saraswathi Dr. Sumalatha Donipati ISBN: 978-93-92760-14-3 Publisher: KY Publications, India KY Publications cordially invites academicians, researchers, faculty members, research scholars, and postgraduate students to contribute original and unpublished book chapters for the forthcoming edited volume, Language, Literature, and Learning: Interdisciplinary Perspectives. This volume aims to provide an interdisciplinary platform for innovative research exploring the intersections of language, literature, education, linguistics, communication, translation, cultural studies, pedagogy, and contemporary learning practices. High-quality theoretical, empirical, and review-based chapters are welcome. Submission Status: Open Publication Fee:2,000 INR / 75US$ per accepted chapter The publication fee includes: Publication of one accepted book chapter One complimentary printed paperback copy of the book Chapter publication certificate (E-Certificate) ISBN-assigned edited book E-chapter publication within 3 days after successful peer review, acceptance, and completion of publication formalities How to Submit Authors are requested to submit their manuscripts as a Microsoft Word (.doc/.docx) attachment via email to: Email: submissiontokypub@gmail.com For Queries and Communication WhatsApp (Text Messages Only): +91 88858 48005 Please Note: Voice calls are not accepted. Kindly communicate only through WhatsApp text messages for submission-related queries and updates. Suggested Themes Authors may submit chapters related to, but not limited to, the following areas: English Language Teaching (ELT) English Literature Comparative Literature Literary Theory and Criticism Linguistics Applied Linguistics Translation Studies Cultural Studies Postcolonial Literature Gender Studies Digital Humanities Discourse Analysis Language and Society Communication Studies Educational Technology Curriculum Development Language Assessment Second Language Acquisition Creative Writing Interdisciplinary Learning Artificial Intelligence in Language Education Digital Learning and Online Education Contemporary Literary Studies Inclusive Education Multilingual Education Language Policy Any other topic relevant to the theme of the book Submission Guidelines Authors should prepare their manuscript according to the following structure: Title Author(s) Name(s) Institutional Affiliation(s) Email Address(es) Abstract (Maximum 200 words) Keywords (3-5) Introduction Methodology (where applicable) Results/Analysis (if applicable) Discussion Conclusion References (APA 7th Edition) Formatting Guidelines Paper Size: A4 Length: Minimum 5 pages - Maximum 15 pages (including references) Font: Times New Roman Font Size: 12 pt Line Spacing: Double Citation Style: APA 7th Edition File Format: Microsoft Word (.doc/.docx) Review and Publication Double-blind peer review process Editorial decision communicated promptly E-Chapter publication within 3 days after successful review, acceptance, and completion of publication formalities Printed book published subsequently Originality Submitted chapters must be original, unpublished, and should not be under consideration for publication elsewhere. All submissions will be screened for plagiarism. Manuscripts with significant plagiarism will be rejected without review. Who Can Submit? We welcome contributions from: Faculty Members Professors Researchers Research Scholars Postgraduate Students Independent Scholars Professionals working in language, literature, education, and related interdisciplinary fields We warmly invite scholars and researchers to contribute to this interdisciplinary volume and become part of a collaborative academic initiative that promotes excellence in language, literature, and learning research.

items.fullDescription: This panel at the SAMLA 97 conference (Nov. 5-7, 2026) examines hospitality as a critical framework within postcolonial studies, attending to the ways literary and cultural texts negotiate welcome, refusal, obligation, and exposure in the aftermath of empire. Within empire studies, hospitality emerges as a fraught ethical and aesthetic relation shaped by colonial histories, uneven mobility, and asymmetrical power. We consider how such texts register these dynamics across postcolonial contexts. Readings across genres and media—including novels, graphic novels, life writing, film, poetry, and theory—deploy hospitality as a vital lens for understanding postcolonial subjectivity, relationality, and the ongoing legacies of colonial governance.

items.fullDescription: Virginia Woolf’s Mrs Dalloway (1925) remains a cornerstone of the modernist literary canon. Often celebrated as Woolf’s greatest novel, the plot is set in post-World War I London and revolves around a single day in the life of its protagonist, Mrs Clarissa Dalloway, as she prepares to host an evening party. Beneath its seemingly simple plot, the novel exemplifies a profound exploration of consciousness, time, and the inner lives of characters through its extensive use of the stream-of-consciousness technique. Although narrated in a third-person omniscient voice, the narrative primarily focuses on the inner consciousness of the characters.

items.fullDescription: In her foundational text Immigrant Acts , cultural theorist Lisa Lowe argues that the Asian immigrant exists as a “perpetual foreigner within the body politic,” a figure through whom the nation-state defines its own boundaries and citizenship. For Lowe, Asian American cultural production emerges precisely from this “distance from the national culture,” serving as a site that critiques the state’s false promises of universal inclusion.

items.fullDescription: This panel invites exploration and interrogation of in(hospitable) spaces (political, affective, spatial) and its relationship to identity and trauma. Since writing is a way of exploring, representing, and healing from trauma, papers exploring how writing functions as a form of hospitality are welcome also. I envision exclusion of certain bodies because of racism, sexism, xenophobia, etc as treating said bodies as intruders. Hence, writing one's silenced, displaced self becomes an act of claiming space and rendering inhospitable spaces hospitable. Zimbabwean writer and activist Tsitsi Dangarembga in Black and Female: Essays (2023) defines empire as trauma that has created ruptures for the people who have experienced colonialism's violence and aftermath. She also attributes writing as a way of healing and belonging for the collective whose identities have been negated by the traumas of racism. This panel invites exploration and interrogation of in(hospitable) spaces (political, affective, spatial) and its relationship to identity and trauma. Since writing is a way of exploring, representing, and healing from trauma, papers exploring how writing functions as a form of hospitality are welcome also. I envision exclusion of certain bodies because of racism, sexism, xenophobia, etc as treating said bodies as intruders. Hence, writing one's silenced, displaced self becomes an act of claiming space and rendering inhospitable spaces hospitable. Possible topics include: · Black women's writing and healing from trauma · Postcolonial ruptures including war and political repression · Refugees, migrants, camps · Racism, alienation, and trauma

items.fullDescription: Setting the stage: a theatre and performance dissertation writing group Writing a dissertation can be lonely: we want to change that. Many of us first fell in love with theater because — through exploratory rehearsals, late-night tech runs, and joyful opening nights — we found camaraderie and connection, both of which can feel distant in graduate school. If theater is best practiced with others, how can our research and writing processes be shaped by the same commitment to community-building?

items.fullDescription: Marianne Moore 2027, hosted by the Faculty of English at the University of Cambridge, will follow the highly successful formats of previous Moore conferences (Penn State 2003, Houston 2015, Buffalo 2020, Stanford 2025) with a schedule of panels, round tables and Q&As for a single audience over two and a half days. Exactly 100 years after Moore herself visited Cambridge, the purpose of the conference is to bring together an international and various group of readers with a strong predilection for thinking and writing about poetry, and put them to work newly and again on this singular, difficult, and discriminating poet. The schedule will take shape around readings between Marianne Moore and other writers.

This session intends to explore the theme of “hospitality” in the works of Joseph Conrad in order to highlight how Conrad’s relationships both reflected and influenced his literary output throughout his career. Some relationships were more enduring than others, but all had an impact, often a profound impact, on his life and writing.

items.fullDescription: Rome and Capri, 21–28 June, 2027 F. Scott Fitzgerald visited Italy on two distinct occasions: first in the spring of 1921 during his and Zelda’s long-deferred honeymoon trip, and again from late 1924 to early 1925 during his unsuccessful attempt to economize. While the Fitzgeralds made brief stops in Venice, San Remo, Portofino, Savona, Pisa, Fiesole, Florence, and Naples, it was in the two host sites of this conference—Rome and Capri—that he spent the most time. It was here that he found inspiration for some of his most celebrated works, as well as several others that remain underexplored.

items.fullDescription: The Department of English-Language Literatures and Cultures and the Institute of Literary Studies at UWM are pleased to invite you to an international interdisciplinary conference on H.G. Wells and Modernity: Texts, Contexts, (Re)visions 22nd-24th Oct. 2026 University of Warmia and Mazury in Olsztyn, Poland Celebrating H.G. Wells's 160th/80th anniversaries of birth and death (1866-1946), this conference invites scholars from across literary studies, linguistics, film studies, political theory, sociology, history, philosophy, and other disciplines to examine modernity's (con)texts through Wells, his contemporaries, and his followers. We aim to consider how their visions reshape understandings of the evolution and devolution of the human, humanity, and society. In many of his literary and non-literary texts, Wells diagnosed the shortcomings of class structures and anticipated modernity's ills, imaginatively prefiguring many current ways of conceptualising the human in relation to the nonhuman. He started as one of the most prolific and versatile short story writers of the late nineteenth century, and he ended as one of the most celebrated sociological and political writers of the first half of the twentieth century. His views, shaped by a complex and troubled faith in science and reason, helped crystallize the formulation of pivotal documents such as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. He died disillusioned that the new world which he had envisioned had not arrived, or worse, frustrated to see his darkest predictions materialise. Undermined and still overlooked as a literary figure by the modernists, who could not see eye to eye with him despite initial friendships, who is H.G. Wells for posterity, that is, our times? In asking this question, we recognize that Wells's legacy cannot be confined to Anglophone literary history alone, and it still speaks urgently to contemporary crises of technology, social fragmentation and political imagination. We especially welcome approaches that could help us position Wells and other modernity/modernist writers within transnational literary networks and explore their influence on and dialogue with world literatures. These topics and others are most welcome, among the proposed conference focus points related to H.G. Wells: ? Author's sources: from antiquity to Jonathan Swift, Charles Dickens, Edgar Allan Poe, and others. ? Modernity and the new ways of writing: short story, scientific romance, science fiction. ? Modern aesthetics and the fictional world: literary and linguistic patterns. Representations of the human and humanity: ecocritical, cognitive, postcolonial, and other postmodern and post-postmodern re-readings. ? Wells and international fantastic and science fiction: comparative perspectives across French (Jules Verne), German, and other contexts. ? Wells's legacy in Polish literature: Stanis?aw Lem and others. ? Dialogues and contentions with Joseph Conrad, E.M. Forster, Virginia Woolf, and Henry James. ? Literary and linguistic depictions of science, catastrophe, and war. ? Shifting cultural concepts: evolution and devolution, tensions of power, ambiguities of colonial appropriation. ? Intermedia reinterpretations: film, graphic novels, games, and other media. ? Wells in translation. ? Political thought and the concept of world state. ? Models of femininity and social change. ? Wells and his engagement with history and the future. ? Utopias and dystopias of Wells and other writers. Proposals for a twenty-minute paper (about 200 words) and a bio note (100 words) should be sent to hgwellsconference@uwm.edu.pl by 10th September 2026. Notification of acceptance by 14th September 2026. Conference fee (non-refundable) payable by 20th September 2026. The account number will be provided upon acceptance. items.fullDescription: Congreso Estética(s) del Horror: Filosofía, Cine y Literatura. Invitación a envío de propuestas. Congreso presencial (English version below) En los últimos años, el terror ha experimentado una renovada visibilidad cultural. El éxito del cine reciente, la consolidación de una auténtica edad de oro de las series televisivas y la aparición de nuevas voces literarias, impulsadas también por el trabajo de editoriales especializadas, evidencian que el horror ocupa hoy un lugar central en el imaginario colectivo y mantiene una extraordinaria capacidad para dialogar con las preocupaciones estéticas, sociales y políticas de cada época. Sin embargo, esta vitalidad cultural no siempre ha encontrado un correlato equivalente en el ámbito académico. Aunque en las últimas décadas los estudios sobre el terror han crecido de forma significativa, todavía persiste un amplio espacio para profundizar en sus implicaciones filosóficas y en los múltiples cruces entre el pensamiento, la literatura y el cine. Con el propósito de contribuir a este campo de investigación, el congreso Estética(s) del horror: filosofía, cine y literatura busca reunir a especialistas interesados en explorar las múltiples relaciones entre el terror y la reflexión filosófica, así como sus manifestaciones en la literatura y el cine. Nos interesan tanto los trabajos que analicen la filosofía del terror -sus categorías estéticas, éticas, metafísicas o políticas- como aquellos que descubran el terror dentro de la propia tradición filosófica, examinando la presencia de lo monstruoso, lo ominoso, el trauma o el horror en autores, problemas o corrientes del pensamiento, desde la Antigüedad hasta la filosofía contemporánea. El congreso aspira, así, a constituirse en un espacio interdisciplinario de diálogo en el que confluyan perspectivas filosóficas, literarias y cinematográficas para pensar el terror no como un género marginal, sino como una herramienta privilegiada para comprender las inquietudes fundamentales de nuestro presente y de nuestra tradición intelectual. El congreso Estética(s) del terror: Filosofía, cine y literatura dará la bienvenida a propuestas relacionadas con alguno de los siguientes ejes temáticos: Filosofía del horror/Estética del horror Horror e intermedialidad Horror y sociedad El congreso está organizado por el grupo de investigación ESCON (Estética contemporánea: arte, política y sociedad) del Departamento de Filosofía y Sociedad de la Universidad Complutense de Madrid (España), por el proyecto UCM 2026 "Historia Conceptual del Tiempo. Crítica y Política" (PR10/26-32656), y por el Grupo Grite (Grupo de Investigacion en Terror) de la Universidad de Buenos Aires (Argentina), tendrá lugar los días 3 y 4 de diciembre de 2026 en la Facultad de Filosofia (edificio A) de la Universidad Complutense de Madrid. Las propuestas de comunicación deberán enviarse a [congresoesteticasdelhorror@gmail.com] hasta el 11 de septiembre de 2026, indicando en el asunto del mensaje: "Estéticas del horror". Cada envío deberá consistir en un único archivo en formato PDF que incluya: Un resumen de la propuesta de hasta 300 palabras. Una breve nota biográfica del autor/a (máximo 150 palabras), con indicación de su afiliación institucional, en su caso. Se aceptarán propuestas en castellano o en inglés. Las comunicaciones tendrán una duración aproximada de 20 minutos. La notificación de aceptación será comunicada a los participantes antes del 30 de septiembre de 2026. Rafael Carrión Arias es Doctor en Filosofía y Doctor en Teoría de la Literatura y Literatura Comparada. Es profesor en el Dpto. de Filosofía y Sociedad en la Universidad Complutense de Madrid (UCM) y miembro del Seminario Nietzsche Complutense. Es autor multiples artículos de investigacion, así como de dos monografías: "Historia de la literatura griega: los orígenes del método genealógico en F. Nietzsche" (Peter Lang, Nueva York, 2020) y "Batman and the Shadows of Modernity: A Critical Genealogy of Contemporary Hero in the Age of Nihilism" (Routledge/Taylor&Francis, London, 2025). Fernando Gabriel Pagnoni Berns es Doctor en Artes y doctorando en Historia. Es profesor en la Facultad de Filosofía y Letras de la Universidad de Buenos Aires (Argentina), donde dicta cursos sobre cine de terror internacional. Dirige GRITE (Grupo de Investigacion sobre el terror) y el proyecto "Civilizacion y barbarie en el cine de terror argentino" y es autor de una monografía sobre la serie española de televisión Historias para no dormir (Universidad de Cádiz, 2020). Ha editado, además, volúmenes dedicados al bicentenario a los cineastas James Wan, Wes Craven y Dario Argento, al giallo italiano (University Press of Mississippi), al cómic de terror (Routledge) y sobre el terror báltico. Asimismo, dirige la colección Terror: Estudios Críticos (Universidad de Cádiz), la primera colección académica en España dedicada exclusivamente a los estudios sobre el género de terror. Call for Papers Aesthetics of Horror: Philosophy, Film, and Literature In-Person Conference In recent years, horror has experienced a remarkable cultural resurgence. The success of contemporary horror cinema, the consolidation of what many have described as a new golden age of television series, and the emergence of new literary voices-supported in part by the work of specialized publishers-all attest to horror's central place in the contemporary cultural imagination and to its enduring capacity to engage with the aesthetic, social, and political concerns of each historical moment. Yet this cultural vitality has not always been matched by an equivalent level of scholarly attention. Although horror studies have expanded considerably over the past decades, significant opportunities remain for further exploring the philosophical dimensions of horror and the many intersections between philosophy, literature, and film. With the aim of contributing to this growing field of research, the conference Aesthetics of Horror: Philosophy, Film, and Literature seeks to bring together scholars interested in examining the manifold relationships between horror and philosophical inquiry, as well as its manifestations across literature and cinema. We welcome contributions that address the philosophy of horror-its aesthetic, ethical, metaphysical, or political dimensions-as well as papers that identify horror within the philosophical tradition itself, examining the presence of the monstrous, the uncanny, trauma, or horror in philosophers, philosophical problems, or schools of thought, from Antiquity to contemporary philosophy. The conference aims to provide an interdisciplinary forum for dialogue in which philosophical, literary, and film studies perspectives converge to explore horror not as a marginal genre, but as a privileged lens through which to understand some of the most pressing concerns of both our contemporary world and our intellectual tradition. Topics The conference welcomes paper proposals addressing (but not limited to) the following themes: Philosophy of Horror / Aesthetics of Horror Horror and Intermediality Horror and Society The conference is jointly organized by ESCON (Contemporary Aesthetics: Art, Politics and Society), a research group within the Department of Philosophy and Society at the Complutense University of Madrid (Spain); the UCM 2026 research project Conceptual History of Time: Critique and Politics (PR10/26-32656); and GRITE (Research Group on Horror) at the University of Buenos Aires (Argentina). The conference will take place on 3-4 December 2026 at the Faculty of Philosophy (Building A), Complutense University of Madrid. Submission Guidelines Paper proposals should be submitted to congresoesteticasdelhorror@gmail.com no later than 11 September 2026, with "Aesthetics of Horror" as the subject line. Submissions should consist of a single PDF file containing: An abstract of no more than 300 words. A brief biographical note (maximum 150 words), including the author's institutional affiliation, where applicable. Proposals may be submitted in English or Spanish. Accepted papers will be allocated 20 minutes for presentation. Applicants will be notified of the outcome of the review process by 30 September 2026. Organizers Rafael Carrión Arias Rafael Carrión Arias holds PhDs in Philosophy and in Literary Theory and Comparative Literature. He is a Lecturer in the Department of Philosophy and Society at the Complutense University of Madrid (UCM) and a member of the Complutense Nietzsche Seminar. He is the author of numerous scholarly articles and two monographs: History of Greek Literature: The Origins of the Genealogical Method in F. Nietzsche (Peter Lang, New York, 2020) and Batman and the Shadows of Modernity: A Critical Genealogy of the Contemporary Hero in the Age of Nihilism (Routledge, 2025). Fernando Gabriel Pagnoni Berns Fernando Gabriel Pagnoni Berns holds a PhD in Arts and is currently completing a PhD in History. He teaches at the Faculty of Philosophy and Letters of the University of Buenos Aires (Argentina), where he offers courses on international horror cinema. He is the director of GRITE (Research Group on Horror) and of the research project Civilisation and Barbarism in Argentine Horror Cinema. He is the author of a monograph on the Spanish television series Historias para no dormir (University of Cádiz, 2020). He has also edited volumes devoted to the bicentenary of Frankenstein; to the filmmakers James Wan, Wes Craven, and Dario Argento; to the Italian giallo (University Press of Mississippi); to horror comics (Routledge); and to Baltic horror. In addition, he serves as the general editor of Horror: Critical Studies (University of Cádiz), the first academic book series in Spain devoted exclusively to horror studies.

items.fullDescription: This virtual seminar takes its inspiration from Saidiya Hartman’s citation of the Greek etymology of the word chorus : “to dance within an enclosure.” For Hartman, the chorus also furnishes a paradigm for thinking—to play on Jean-Luc Nancy’s notion—Black (non)being singular plural. In Wayward Lives , Hartman suggests that any of the minor figures can stand in for the others, not as a claim of homogeneity, but rather as a contention that their singularity can be read as paradigmatic of waywardness—in its queer heterogeneity—in general. The chorus of black femme and queer youth is thus at once a singular plurality and a plural singularity as they make beautifully riotous lives within the enclosure of social death.

items.fullDescription: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that separate cinema, literature, and the visual arts. Factors including the rise of globalized societies and the emergence of new technology have forced us to reconsider medium specificity and our relationship with creative expressions and works of art.

items.fullDescription: This panel session seeks to explore the cultural fascination surrounding "The Backrooms," an internet 'creepypasta' about an abandoned office building with endless corridors that became a popular YouTube series and most recently, a blockbuster horror film. What are the Backrooms? Do they represent an artificial or alien intelligence attempting to simulate reality? Are they a glitch in the imperfect fabric of an unstable universe? Are they a metaphor for the subconscious, techno-paranoia, wasteland necrocapitalism, corporate control, or the internet itself? Or is this all just a retelling of the Greek myth of the Labyrinth for a Gen Z audience?

items.fullDescription: This roundtable discusses the convergence of literary expression and digital activism as means of empowering a community. The shift of passive representation to active agency in the 21st century can be common in the digital space where the local knowledge is stored and disseminated. We discuss how marginalized groups, namely Dalit writers and activists, have used digital humanities to evade authoritarian gatekeepers and develop transnational solidarities. This session welcomes people to talk about how online spaces can be seen as a form of regenerative space where the testimonios of Dalit can be heard, transforming the world into a supportive space of collective action and literary insurgency.

items.fullDescription: On January 19, 2022, Sheila Heti published the first of ten weekly installments of “A Diary in Alphabetical Order” in The New York Times. Each installment included an author’s note explaining that Heti had “loaded all 500,000 words of [her] journals into Excel to order the sentences alphabetically,” hoping to “see the self in a new way.” Over time, the project “grew into something more novelistic,” and the note concludes that “the self’s report on itself is surely a great fiction.” Heti is hardly the first author to challenge or destabilize autobiographical conventions.

items.fullDescription: Drawing inspiration from Adrienne Rich's seminal book Of Woman Born (1976), in 2006, Andrea O'Reilly coined the term "Motherhood Studies" and established a new interdisciplinary academic field aimed at studying "motherhood" as an institution and "mothering" as an experience. O'Reilly emphasized the importance of adopting a feminist framework designed expressly for mothers: a "matricentric feminism" necessary for an intersectional exploration of motherhood. Within "Motherhood Studies," being or not being a mother matters, and the experience of motherhood is treated as a category alongside gender, class, and race.

Building upon Hortense Spillers’ important essay Mama’s Baby, Papa’s Maybe (1987), we seek papers from humanities scholars in the French and Francophone worlds that reframe the body not as a passive victim but as a living text, a site of memory, and an alternative space that rescripts official records. By retracing histories through the reading of the body, we can activate affect, unleash unexpected memories, and create generative spaces for art that centres on cultural knowledge to empower communities.

items.fullDescription: Panel Proposal for the NeMLA Convention, Newport, RI, March 6–9, 2027 Planetary crises—climate change, toxic pollution, biodiversity loss, and energy transition—are profoundly uneven in their distribution. Their material consequences are shaped by infrastructures of class, race, colonial histories, state power, and global capitalism. Increasingly, literary, artistic, and cultural texts across the world foreground these asymmetries, framing ecological crisis not only as environmental catastrophe but as a question of justice: Who breathes polluted air? Who profits from green transformation? Who consumes melting ice? Who bears the social and environmental costs of planetary change?

items.fullDescription: The 1995 film Smoke, directed by Wayne Wang, features characters who band together by frequenting a corner cigar store in Brooklyn. Paul Auster, who wrote the screenplay, once described the community his characters created as a "little universe for themselves that is not determined by blood or the past." Members of traditional, fixed communities often belong because of generational roots and longtime residency. This panel session will explore smaller, and often more liminal, communities that are forged through other means. In such communities, membership is not dependent on family ties or connection to place. This community is one of commonality: members come together through common needs, interests, traumas, or goals. They may be temporary or long lasting, healthy or dysfunctional, organized or informal. Questions to consider might include, but are not limited to, the following: Under what circumstances do these "little universe" communities form, and what is their function? How do they fit (or not fit) into the larger community, and how do they reflect or perhaps indict the larger community? How do they transform the lives of their members? What unexpected effects do the groups have on their members? What happens when these communities disband? Possible examples of these communities: Support groups Virtual communities Groups that form in institutions (colleges, prisons) Clubs and groups of people with similar interests (book clubs, game-playing clubs, hiking groups, knitting circles, etc.) Communities formed of exclusion/communities of outsiders This panel welcomes papers that examine prose fiction, poetry, film, documentary, nonfiction, and culture. Paper proposals are accepted through September 30, 2026. Abstracts are 200-300 words in length, and titles should not exceed 100 characters. Please submit your abstract at the session's portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22409 Modality: This session is in hybrid format (in person, but will accept remote presentations). For more information on the convention and to view NeMLA's guidelines for abstracts, visit https://www.nemla.org Questions regarding the panel may be addressed to Lori Cummings at LoNewco1@wsc.edu

items.fullDescription: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or negotiate traditional ideologies of the “good” mother as self-sacrificing, nurturing, and sexless with her antithesis, the “bad” mother. In the last few years, we have seen the rise of matrifocal writings published to break taboos about previously silenced reproductive experiences, create a sense of community, support, and care, and, overall, empower people.

She continues by explaining her personal experience with this spatiality, noting that, “Like many of my contemporaries I have yearned to find my place in this world, to have a sense of homecoming, a sense of being wedded to a place” (2). To that end, a commonality shared between all humans is a searching for a place in which we belong, and this is particularly evident in literature that is produced during times of major societal events.

items.fullDescription: In her resolute commitment to telling stories about Black life without the white gaze, Toni Morrison articulated a model of community empowerment grounded in local knowledge, intra-communal collaboration, and the self-reflexive narration of Black worlds. While Morrison remains one of the most visible public advocates of this aesthetic and ethical orientation, practices of Black worldmaking across African and diasporic literatures have long exceeded mere response to anti-Blackness. Even under conditions structured by racial violence, coloniality, displacement, and social rupture, Black communities have continually generated modes of life, relation, memory, and imagination that assert the possibility of worlds otherwise.

items.fullDescription: Since 1991 the Pulitzer Prize has been awarded to four novels that reimagined literary works that have long been a part of the canon. They are: A Thousand Acres by Jane Smiley (a contemporary King Lear ); March by Geraldine Brooks ( Little Women from the perspective of the absent father); Demon Copperhead by Barbara Kingsolver (David Copperfield set in Appalachia); and James by Percival Everett (Adventures of Huckleberry Finn from the perspective of Jim). This panel looks to open a discussion about how these and other contemporary novels both explore and challenge the themes of the source material. What do they say about society, race, gender, feminism, and marginalization?

items.fullDescription: In both her life and her literary work, American writer Edith Wharton was a keen observer and recorder of the interrelationship(s) between power and community at the end of the 19th century and throughout the early decades of the 20th. Born into the world of wealth and privilege of “Old New York” society, Wharton grew weary of its longstanding communal norms and constraints. At the same time, however, she was also openly critical of the ostentation and conspicuous consumption on display during the annual Newport, RI “summer season.”

items.fullDescription: Beecroft argues in The Cambridge Companion to the Novel that, if we understand the novel as storytelling in any narrative form indigenous to a particular culture or people, then we must acknowledge the existence of multiple novels, each with distinct temporal and spatial origins. He maintains that storytelling traditions are embedded in every society, and that many of these traditions have complemented, intersected with, or diverged from Western narrative forms (46–48). However, the narrative form canonized as the “novel” in the Western literary tradition has a specific historical and aesthetic origin in ancient Greece.

items.location: Newport, Rhode Island · Northeast Modern Language Association
items.excerpt: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, "autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves." In recent times, "life writing" has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, sl...
items.fullDescription: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, "autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves." In recent times, "life writing" has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, slave narratives, prison narratives, captivity narratives, travel narratives, confessionals, conversion narratives and even in recent times, personal diaries. However, rather than remain transparent records of lived experience, life writings actively shape identities, produce historical knowledge, preserve cultural memory, and intervene in social and political worlds. This panel invites scholars to reconsider what life writing does beyond just what it says. We invite contributions that challenge conventional approaches to reading life writings. We are particularly interested in scholarships that examine how life writings preserve marginalized histories, challenge dominant archives, and create alternative ways of knowing. We also welcome papers that investigate life writing across historical periods, geographical locations, and disciplinary approaches. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: Life writing as archive. Counter-archive or alternative historical record. Testimony, witnessing, and the ethics of narration. Life writing and the production of race, gender, sexuality, or class. Spiritual, religious, and ritual dimensions of autobiographical expression. Collective, communal, and relational forms of selfhood. Life writing and memory studies. Digital, visual, and multimodal life narratives. Abstracts of 250-300 words and a short bio (100 words) should be submitted via: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22538 Session modality:Hybrid (in-person but accepting remote presentations).

items.fullDescription: CFP for NeMLA 2027 Conference Virtual Only Panel Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature If Empire thrives and even conquers through written text: codification of laws and bureaucracy, cartography of territory, travelogues of the exotic Other, it equally seeks to suppress oral indigenous knowledge of the vanquished. Yet orality can also be the site of resistance and community identity for just such a reason. This panel seeks proposals of works that investigate how orality of form and/or content in Anglophone literature disrupts Empire and empowers community. The irony of pursuing orality in literature, which is historically written, is not lost on us, but also provides an opportunity for creative, resilient exploration. Some possible ways of approaching this topic may include: Accent and dialect Pidgins, creoles, and contact languages Translations and translators Language and identity Oral folk wisdom and stories Glossaries and indices Textual hybridity or multimodality Literacy This is a Call for Papers for the NeMLA 2027 Conference. items.excerpt: Affect theory has grown largely within Euro-American frameworks, from Berlant's neoliberal America to Stewart's account of the American ordinary. items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a likely RCID fit across Digital Humanities / Design, Theory, Art / Media Practice.
items.fullDescription: Affect theory has grown largely within Euro-American frameworks, from Berlant's neoliberal America to Stewart's account of the American ordinary. Sara Ahmed's work on the cultural politics of emotion has been pivotal in reorienting the field toward race, migration, and colonial power, showing how feelings attach differently to racialized and gendered bodies. This panel builds on that reorientation. It asks what happens when affect theory travels more fully into postcolonial and diasporic contexts, where the body in question has been shaped by colonial histories, forced migration, and asymmetrical encounters with Western institutions. How do structures of feeling shift under these conditions, and where do existing frameworks reach their limit? The panel takes postcolonial and diasporic texts as active interlocutors, capable of pressuring, revising, and extending theory rather than simply illustrating it. We invite papers that attend to the specific conditions under which feeling circulates, accumulates, and fails to discharge in migrant and postcolonial experience. How does testimony feel from the position of the postcolonial subject? What does care cost in communities shaped by colonial injury and displacement? Objects, spaces, and bodies carry affective residue across cultures and histories in ways that ask for new forms of attention and recognition. Papers might engage fiction, film, digital media, and visual culture from postcolonial and diasporic traditions, attending to the affective dimensions of migrant and colonial experience. We welcome contributions grounded in affect theory, postcolonial studies, comparative literature, and media studies. Graduate researchers and faculty are encouraged to apply.

items.fullDescription: In Aldous Huxley's Brave New World, humans are conditioned to seek out human companionship and to despise being by themselves. Helmholtz Watson, a lecturer of Emotional Engineering, relates a story to Bernard Marx about being disciplined for sharing some of his poems with the students he was paid to indoctrinate. When asked what the poems had been about, Helmholtz tells a surprised Bernard, "They were about being alone." Helmholtz, like many individuals in our world outside the novel, has a natural inclination to seek solitude. Some individuals choose to separate from community for short or long periods of time, retreating into the solitude of reflection and quiet. The flip side of solitude is loneliness, occurring when individuals experience unwanted distance from community and feel the lack of human connection. And then, there is an in-between state of those who simultaneously, and seemingly paradoxically, need copious alone time while also desiring community. This creative session will showcase readings of original works of poetry and prose by writers who compose about being alone, whether from a standpoint of loneliness, solitude, or somewhere in between. Poetry, fiction, and creative nonfiction are welcome in this session. Readers may also present a combination of genres. Proposals are accepted through September 30, 2026. Abstracts are 200-300 words in length, and titles should not exceed 100 characters. Please submit your abstract at the session's portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22377 Modality: This session is in hybrid format (in person, but will accept remote presentations). items.fullDescription: This panel presents a review of the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny, Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio. The panel centers on aspects of African diasporic experiences, i.e., Afro-Latinidad. The panel considers Blackness as gleaned and embedded from Bad Bunny's musical repertoire and uses Aurora Santiago Ortiz and Jorell Melendez-Badillo's framework of "historical memory" and "limits of nostalgia" as presented in their piece "(Nada es) 'Como antes.'" The panel seeks to utilize this framework and focus on Black presence in all its intersecting and non-intersecting forms involving race, class, gender, and sex. Specifically, papers are sought that examine strategies and uses of, for example, an "idyllic past" and/or "Great Mixed Family" motif and show ruptures in representations that subsume or otherize Blackness. Submissions can interpret outside of Bad Bunny and include other artists venturing into its use in other realms and/or aspects of appreciation or appropriation discourse, read broadly and sublimated in text. The panel discusses the uses of various musical, visual, aural, and literary works to deconstruct language use, song beats, films, concerts, and media, similar to Bad Bunny's use of House, Hip Hop, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, Bachata, Afro Punk, etc. A key element is locating, gleaning, or demystifying aspects of Blackness, construed broadly, in Bad Bunny's context or in other works that offer perspectives on Black embeddedness and involve authenticity, appreciation, and the intersection of race, class, gender, or sex. Papers are sought that review the application, infusion, or parallelism of the Black past infusing Bomba, Plena, Son, Salsa, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, and other genres, as merged running threads. This panel invites papers that explore the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny (Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio). We focus on African diasporic, or AfroLatinidad, experiences. The panel examines how Blackness construed in various forms appears in Bad Bunny's music and/or is subsumed within it. The panel delves into the intersections of race, class, gender, and sex, and the recognition of the African presence, identity, or representation.

items.fullDescription: In Spring of 2026, the first stage of NASA's Artemis mission captured photographs of the Earth from space, a collective earthling selfie that momentarily transcended the squalor and congestion of the daily news cycle. While satellite imagery, Google maps, and weather forecasting technology have made representations of the global scale relatively commonplace, the astronaut photos have a human presence that allows them to capture something of the sublimity of what it means to live on a planet. The nature of the planetary perspective is epistemologically elusive, being difficult and maybe impossible to reconcile with the phenomenology of lived experience. It is also inherently polysemous, being open to numerous, ramifying, and conflicting interpretations, indicating both mankind's puniness and its awesome technological power, providing a meta-explanation for the reality of everything while being simultaneously abstract and largely irrelevant to everyday life, suggesting a deep connection between human beings and Earth while also overriding Earth toward the project of discovering new and improved planetary homes. Gayatri Chakrovarty Spivak positions inherent alterity at the center of what she calls "planet-thought": "The planet is in the species of alterity, belonging to another system; and yet we inhabit it, on loan. … If we imagine ourselves as planetary subjects … alterity remains underived from us; it is not our dialectical negation, it contains us as much as it pushes us away." Cinema is the art of representing the world through an uncanny simulacrum of itself, which may make it a particularly apt medium for capturing the ambivalent and paradoxical alterity that defines the ontology of the planetary imagination. Indeed, one of the first works of narrative cinema, Georges Méliès's A Trip to the Moon (1902), is structured around visionary depictions of Earth from space that uncannily anticipate the Apollo and Artemis photos while also inspiring a rich filmic legacy of representations of planetarity. The narrative and imagery of such films has helped to shape the popular perception of what it means to live on a planet, and at their best, these films engage the kinds of contradictions, paradoxes, and ambiguities that Spivak and other cultural studies theorists have explored. This panel will focus on what movies about planets can teach us about what planets are and how to live on them. We accept proposals about movies that foreground telluric-scale events on earth (Armageddon [1998], Don't Look Up [2021], etc.), movies about space exploration (Apollo 13 [1995], Gravity [2013], The Martian [2015], etc.), or movies set on alien planets (Star Wars, Dune, etc.). Any other approaches to examining the intersection between cinema and planetarity are also welcome. Please submit a 300-word abstract by September 30, 2026. Contact Dr. Randy Laist with questions: rlaist@bridgeport.edu.

items.fullDescription: Accepting Submissions for NeMLA Roundtable: In this in-person roundtable conference session, we are looking to open conversation about the unique ways that educators are innovating with forms of gaming, play, and playfulness in the classroom. We are interested in discussing the incorporation of any and all forms of games and gaming, including, but not limited to: - board, tabletop, and card games - social deduction games - tabletop role-playing systems such as Dungeons and Dragons - invented role-playing scenarios - gamification of in-class activities - video and digital games Abstract / Overview Tabletop gaming and literary studies share a core essence: storytelling. As we aim to teach students in our writing, rhetoric, composition, and literature courses the values and techniques of narrative, we might choose to borrow from the myriad strategies developed in the world of board and role-playing games in order to engage learners on an even more visceral level. Applying approaches of play and pretend, whether through published or in-class created gaming scenarios, educators can enable rich modes of experiential learning. Such learning has the potential to bond our classroom communities more profoundly than verbal chatter alone may accomplish-and, perhaps, to undergird our intrinsic human need to build shared, lived encounters in ways that machines and AI cannot simulate. Submission Now accepting applications. Please submit abstracts for consideration via the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22656 Deadline September 30, 2026

items.title: Liminal Dialogues: AI, Voice, and Impossible Conversations in Literary Creation
items.location: Newport, RI Convention Site: Salve Regina University · NEMLA
items.excerpt: This creative session invites scholars, writers, and hybrid practitioner-theorists to explore liminal dialogue as a form of literary creation in which unlikely, displaced, or structurally incompatible voices are brought into conversation. Drawing on the centrality of dialogue in literary and dramatic traditions, this panel reimagines conversational form a...
items.fullDescription: This creative session invites scholars, writers, and hybrid practitioner-theorists to explore liminal dialogue as a form of literary creation in which unlikely, displaced, or structurally incompatible voices are brought into conversation. Drawing on the centrality of dialogue in literary and dramatic traditions, this panel reimagines conversational form as a site of constraint-based experimentation, where meaning emerges through the collision of discourses that would not ordinarily coexist. Rather than focusing on artificial intelligence as a technological subject, this session treats AI-assisted generation as a formal device for producing estranged adjacency between voices, genres, and epistemologies. What happens when characters drawn from radically different narrative worlds, historical moments, or discursive systems are placed in structured conversation? How does meaning shift when dialogue is detached from stable context, shared reality, or conventional narrative logic? We welcome creative-critical submissions that use AI-assisted or procedural methods to generate liminal dialogues between incompatible speakers or speech forms. Contributions might include conversations between fictional, historical, or conceptual figures who would not normally meet; dialogues staged between competing textual systems or genres; or experimental works in which voice, tone, and argumentative logic destabilize one another through forced proximity. Submissions may also explore the failure of communication itself as a productive literary space. The session is especially interested in work that frames dialogue as a site of interpretive tension rather than narrative coherence, and in which meaning emerges through friction, misalignment, and discursive instability. AI or generative systems may be used as compositional tools, but the emphasis should remain on literary form, voice construction, and interpretive effect rather than technology itself. We encourage submissions across poetry, prose, hybrid writing, and performative or experimental formats suitable for conference presentation.

items.fullDescription: In the four hundred years since its invention in Renaissance Florence, opera has become synonymous with the grandiose, the excessive, and the melodramatic, yet it has only gained a foothold in the academy as an object of serious academic study within the past fifty years. Since then, however, an abundance of scholarship has yielded everything from formal musicological readings of operatic works to theoretical inquiries inspired by psychoanalysis into voice and performance. And topics like the relationship between opera and sovereignty in seventeenth century Italy and the appropriation of Wagner by the Third Reich underscore how opera has never been far from the political sphere in the Western world. But as Slavoj Zizek confirms, "the more opera is dead, the more it flourishes." Opera's story may be one of perpetual decadence, but contemporary creators deserving of academic attention push against the narrative and economic forces that have challenged the validity of opera as an artform in the 21st century. This panel seeks to complement contemporary scholarship on opera across the humanities by soliciting papers that speak to opera's unique, overdetermined, and intrinsically interdisciplinary blend of words, music, performance, and history, as well as papers that examine operatic genealogies extant in non-operatic texts. Potential panelists are also invited to think across languages, borders, and performance traditions and consider the important social and political questions of canonicity, representation, and ownership as they pertain to opera and the operatic.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers -- NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention. Link: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22237 March 6-9, 2027. Newport, Rhode Island. Convention Site: Salve Regina University. Session Abtract The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: "man is a political animal because he is a literary animal," the fundamental condition of which is the "excess of words" constitutive of the human animal's politicalness. Literature (or poetry) has long been placed in close proximity to politics, as we find in Plato's Republic, where philosophy and literature-and, more generally, philosophy and politics-turn out to be perennial adversaries. That matters of literature bear upon the question of politics has featured in continental French thought for quite some time, considered in terms of the milieu of différance and event in Jacques Derrida; le partage du sensible and dissensus in Jacques Rancière; and the unpresentable and testimony to le différend in Jean-François Lyotard, among others. More recently, Geoffrey Bennington has taken up the question of the unreadable as that condition that keeps reading open to the event of alterity. What is at stake, then, in the complex relationship between literature and politics? How does literature bear witness to, or otherwise reconfigure, who counts as a speaking being, what is recognized as speech, and/or what falls out of discursive legibility as mere noise, excess, or silence? And how might mis-reading, mis-translation, and mis-understanding contribute to the politics of literature? This panel invites reflection on the figures, scenes, and events of textuality that respond to these and related questions. All critical approaches are welcome for this in-person panel. Eric J. Adamo McMaster University Hamilton, Ontario, Canada.

items.fullDescription: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their divergent perspectives, or their transgression of established norms, certain characters find themselves pushed to the margins of social, familial, or professional groups. While such exclusion may appear to weaken those who experience it, this panel asks whether marginalization might also function as a source of strength. Can marginality become a generative position? Do marginalized individuals succeed in forming new communities that enable them to confront stigma and ultimately overcome it? This discussion seeks to shift our understanding of the place occupied by marginalized individuals and groups in fictional narratives. By considering the networks of solidarity that emerge among marginalized subjects, the panel explores what lies beneath their presumed inferiority, inviting critical attention to what is hidden, erased, or rendered invisible within dominant narratives. More importantly, we will examine how marginalized communities may become empowering spaces of resistance, mutual support, and hope. Drawing on approaches from cultural studies, postcolonial theory, sociolinguistics, and intersectionality, this session seeks to understand how literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic narratives stage the tensions between marginality and belonging, strength and vulnerability. Proposals engaging with environmental humanities, posthumanism, Indigenous studies, as well as diaspora and migration studies are strongly encouraged. We also welcome submissions drawing on gender and queer studies, memory and trauma studies, digital humanities, or other interdisciplinary frameworks within the humanities. We welcome proposals of 250-300 words, in French or English, submitted by September 30.

items.fullDescription: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part of our cultural imagination, but it's less easy to coax students into reading Beowulf or Tom Jones -texts that are less familiar in form and content. As we jettison the canon and the timeless truths of literature, what do we expect reading older texts will give our students that they wouldn't otherwise understand? How do we explain our reasoning to students about why they should read texts from earlier literary periods? How do we present these earlier periods (or periodization) and their culture and ideas as relevant to our undergraduate students in our English, writing, or humanities programs? Or is relevance the problem? How might we redefine older literature in our classes, or what we (can) do with these texts? I'm particularly interested in position papers that take into consideration practical pedagogical and theoretical concerns: arguments about curricula, course development or assignments that do different things with this literature. Papers are welcome on any kind of literature/texts or periods prior to 1800.

items.fullDescription: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. However, several personal narratives resist these assumptions. Edited autobiographies, oral histories, collaborative memoirs, and interview-based narratives often construct an autobiographical "I"/protagonist that is multiple rather than singular and that emerges across the voices of individuals whose experiences collectively narrate a shared history. Likewise, some collective life narratives depart from conventional autobiography structures, appearing as fragmented accounts, interwoven testimonies, or dispersed chapters within larger texts. This panel invites papers that examine forms of self-representation that foreground the collective. Particularly of interest are papers that interrogate the presumed unity of author, narrator, and protagonist; reconsider the autobiographical pact in light of collaborative and collective forms of narration; and explore how communities write themselves into history through shared acts of remembrance, testimony, and self-representation. Topics to consider include, but are not limited to: Collaborative and edited autobiographies Oral histories and interview-based life narratives Community and collective testimony Indigenous, Black, and diasporic collective life writing Gender, Sexuality and self testaments Archives, memory, and community self-representation Theoretical reconsiderations of the autobiographical pact and authorial voice Experimental and hybrid forms of autobiography Collective authorship, voice, and narrative authority Life writing, activism, and community formation. By centering collective modes of self-account, this panel seeks to expand the boundaries of autobiography studies and establish interventions that consider how communities, rather than individuals alone, become autobiographical subjects.

items.fullDescription: Philip Kan Gotanda's play Yankee Dawg You Die centers a conflict between two Asian American actors. The aging Vincent Chang built his career playing bumbling Japanese soldiers and one-dimensional Asian stereotypes in classic Hollywood. He's confronted by the up-and-coming Bradley Yamashita, who insists that Asian (American) characters should be played with dignity and accuracy. The heart of the play is an ambivalence about Asian (American) representation and whether viewers should sympathize with Chang for taking the only acting jobs available or with Yamashita in his condemnation of the racist roles-or with both. This panel seeks to explore comparable ambivalences in Asian/diasporic representation. We specifically seek papers about contemporary attempts to re-negotiate or reclaim depictions of Asian people, culture, literature, myth, history, etc. The papers may examine any form of media and utilize any theoretical lens, as long as the media in question seek to address anti-Asian racism or stereotypes, or to explore ambiguities in Asian/diasporic experience. "Asian" for the purposes of this panel, includes anyone (descended) from the Asian continent. By challenging imagery and cultural conceptions that stereotype or limit Asian people, this panel intends to engage the conference theme by exploring empowerments of Asian communities, whether in Asia or abroad. Please submit a 200-300 word abstract at https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22330 by 30 Sept.

items.fullDescription: Language is key to communication, whether visual, auditory, linguistic, spatial, or gestural. In literature, modalities might include devices such as phraseology, tone, sensory elements, symbolism, satire, irony, characterizations, or figurative language. Modalities in literature can be narrative, descriptive, persuasive, expository, causal, effectual, definitive, comparative, and argumentative. Through close and careful examination of "voice," "word," or "non-verbal expressions" of speaker or writer, someone might discover surprising probabilities, poignant certainties, unexpected opportunities, awakened obligations, absurd necessities, or unusual permissions that shift from "a state of passivity to being active agents of change." This roundtable session will explore, define, determine, and discuss how key modalities in various literatures have the ability to excite, intrigue, and empower communities--large and small, academic and personal, collective and individual-to values of positivity and collectiveness.

items.fullDescription: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-Noir exhibit at the Pompidou- and what Gertrude Stein calls the Lost Generation to find empowerment in exile. We seek to share techniques of research and teaching about the power of the powerless by studying overlooked artistic communities, individuals and cultures. For example we welcome submissions about how artists of the Paris Noir and/or Lost Generation relate to issues of culture, creating and community in our time. Many are unaware that James Baldwin, Miles Davis, and Jean-Michel Basquiat worked alongside Ernest Hemingway, Gertrude Stein and Henry Miller. These expatriates and African American artists electively inhabited the creative "Undercommons" of Paris Noir to remain generative despite fascism, racism, and war. These artists who were foreigners and outsiders are now classic and read inside the classroom by a majority in culture. Submit papers that analyze minorities, foreigners, strangers, exiles and outsiders in literature, film, or culture as a model for reflecting upon ways of reterritorializing the classroom and community. Below are some questions to orient your submission, although you are not limited to working on these: How does the marginalized turn the margin to a source of empowerment? What type of artist or work demonstrates change on the margins? What does it mean to inhabit to the role of foreigner, outsider, or minority? How does the existence of the stranger, immigrant, minority, or neighbor facilitate cross-cultural dialogue and generativity? How do we inhabit and engage vulnerability, process, child-like discovery, repetition and outside as a way of innovating that resists an authoritarian or artificial rule? Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22549 Chair: Dr. Jessica Datema, jdatema@bergen.edu, https://bergen.edu/academics/academic-divisions-departments/english-department/our-composition-literature-faculty/jessica-datema/ https://www.nemla.org/convention.html
